item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If instances are appearing outside of containment, then it's not Safe, it's Euclid.

Especially since a containment breach would kill people, and could lead to a much bigger issue if the problems were ever leaked to the public.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You had me at "a rough cube of human body parts"
I like my Maths Skips to be visceral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All my remaining will to do Math is extinct.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting concept with good execution to match.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As someone who solves math problems, I say "boy, am I glad Soviet books were not affected!"

The tricky bit about the "subpar" D-Class - if they needed a computer to help calculate the answer, what type of comprehension of the answer is the trigger? I realize that for the Foundation it was sufficient to determine "dangerous, do not solve" is containment enough, but still. Is it enough merely knowing the answer is 42, or understanding why 42 makes the stupid pun in the problem work, for example?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Disturbing.

I like it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I fucking knew math was bad news. +1, I hate to think of where they go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I seem to have jumped onto an SCP that has had part of it removed. Without the "incident" to which others seem to be referring it actually enhanced the article for me. It makes the mind wonder as to why the bodies arrive in cubes without the brain or nervous system. Obviously they are being harvested, but why are the intelligent individuals returned in such a neat manner. Also, were they alive during the removal process? Much to wonder about.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mars needs brains! +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty neat. Seems to have been cleaned up a lot from the previous versions, judging by the comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh god, some of those are my textbooks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When a source is found to be contaminated, a complete recall is to be issued through appropriate channels and all copies are to be destroyed by incineration.

So does the word problem just show up in the books after getting published? If so, you should note that. If not, then beyond just recalling the books they'd need to get the publisher to take that problem out of their manuscript.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really not a fan of the idea that it persuades you to solve the problem through unknown means. I also agree with Scantron that the testlog didn't add anything to this. Other than that I like the concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The item itself doesn't grab me conceptually, either. So something is harvesting brain tissue from people who pass the math problem intelligence test. Why does it bother sending back scary mangled corpses with cliched "written on skin" messages? Wouldn't that be completely counterproductive? It obviously wants more people to read the calculus problem, which is probably not going to happen if the textbook happens to be next to some crazy cubified corpse thing. The whole thing feels completely tacked-on for the purposes of horror.

That's kind of why I like it, actually. At some point, somewhere, maybe even NOW, someone in our world is busy harvesting brains for this extra-dimensional thing. They're not in constant communication, either, and the only way to talk is one-way. Who knows why, that's just the laws of their communication. And why are the bodies cubic? Disposal, use in a furnace or something else? I don't believe that this was author oversight, it was an invitation to explore the mystery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, someone didn't do too well on their Calculus finals…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Test log is pretty much unnecessary, otherwise I like it. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fucking sweet

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For the year, I admit that I wasn't really sure which year of the 60s to put it in, and the foundation tends to expunge dates and names. Dr. A is the person in charge of the project, but I guess that portion isn't really all that important.

I can get rid of the SCP division, since honestly that's not particularly important and it'll probably read better without it. I'll do that right now, actually.

The idea behind it sending back corpses and them having messages on them is that it implies they're meant for someone. Obviously the problems are getting into textbooks somehow, and someone's developing more of them. In the "wild", so to speak, there are probably agents of another party who these are intended for, and they could possibly be covering up the situation. The other party may not have anything to do with the pieces, or they don't want to remove too much important material, or they feel it's contamination of the world or time line or dimension to just remove a whole body, or something.

The text logs are mostly just to prove what's stated in the description, since I got several questions of "how do we know?" when I presented it as a draft. I could say "testing has proven x" or I could show tests that explain exactly why it works. These essentially allow us to prove some statements, with explanations for why this tells us these things.

At least part of it is that people liked it better when it was more mysterious; when you explain something like this too much, it's even less interesting than it is now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't care for this one. I don't like the expungement at all (why do you need to redact the last numeral of the year this appeared? Or the edition of textbooks this appears in, since you've already blackboxed the title?), the fact that someone has to go looking for a specific blackboxed doctor to test this thing, or the latest iteration of "lol the Foundation always uses SCP as an acronym get it?".

The item itself doesn't grab me conceptually, either. So something is harvesting brain tissue from people who pass the math problem intelligence test. Why does it bother sending back scary mangled corpses with cliched "written on skin" messages? Wouldn't that be completely counterproductive? It obviously wants more people to read the calculus problem, which is probably not going to happen if the textbook happens to be next to some crazy cubified corpse thing. The whole thing feels completely tacked-on for the purposes of horror.

The test logs also feel like filler, and don't really tell us anything that we didn't already get from the article other than that whatever it is that's processing these people doesn't like it when you send them the non-mathematically inclined.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You could change that first portion if you really want to. I personally think it's fine as is, but if you want it to be consistent with other safe objects, that's your own call. I don't think either one will cause you a downvote.

As instances of SCP-1889 are not a threat when left unsolved, containment revolves around the removal and destruction of all contaminated material. When a source is found to be contaminated, a complete recall is to be issued through appropriate channels and all copies are to be destroyed by incineration1. A Foundation front company, Stateside Calculus Processors, has been created for the express purpose of monitoring and removing instances of the object from before they reach circulation.

And you NEED that part. I mean, this thing keeps showing up in the wild. That's _almost_ enough to make it Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is indeed my first SCP. I've watched for a few years now, though, and made sure to get it looked at as a draft first, so I think I managed to avoid most of the big pitfalls. I think there are ways to improve it, especially near the end, but I'm pretty happy with the current form.

I'm debating changing the containment procedures, though. It's a safe object that doesn't do anything if you don't mess with it; maybe all the instances can simply go into a standard Safe object containment locker?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is perfect now… is this your first skip? If so, I am thoroughly impressed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright, I've removed -1, since it didn't really add too much to the article. Glad that it seems okay as it is, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I agree with the above. I enjoyed this piece until I hit the SCP-1 incident. I think you could get rid of that whole thing entirely and it would be a much better, creepier article. Right now it just drags the whole thing down.

I still enjoyed it though , so upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree it was really interesting until I got to the Incident which felt like it was added so there would be some violence on the piece. I will have to downvote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not bad, up until the hunter-killer machine appeared. That section not only felt tacked on, but the expungement was sloppy. No vote for now.
